column5: Easy for web devs — plugin architecture, runs in any browser
column8: Any static web host (GitHub Pages, own server), or dedicated free services like Cognition.run and self-hosted JATOS; Pavlovia also supported
column9: No fixed UI language; all text is developer-supplied, so any language works
column10: No built-in tasks; large plugin/contrib ecosystem (jspsych-contrib) covers Stroop, Flanker, IAT, N-back, visual search, surveys, etc. — it's the substrate most JS batteries above are built on

column6: Easy via graphical Builder, full Python scripting for complex logic
column9: Pavlovia.org (official, credit- or institutional-license based) or self-host on any web server since PsychoJS output is plain JS/HTML
column10: GUI has an active Arabic localization effort (2025.1.0); experiment content/instructions are author-defined in any language
column11: No fixed battery; huge published-experiment ecosystem (visual/auditory psychophysics, RT tasks, surveys); free hosting via Pavlovia (not open-source itself)

column5: Easy — drag-and-drop GUI + optional Python inline scripting; plugins for eye-trackers, etc.
column8: Export to OSWeb format and deploy through a self-hosted JATOS server (most common route), or any web server that can serve the OSWeb JS bundle
column10: No fixed battery; widely used to build classic RT/cognitive paradigms

column5: Moderate — code-only (no GUI builder), runs on Android too
column11: No fixed battery; library for building precise-timing experiments

column5: Harder — requires MATLAB/Octave coding, steepest learning curve of the group
column6: Historically MIT-style (mixed, no single SPDX license); since v3.0.20, Windows/macOS builds require a paid license key (free 14-day trial) — Linux builds remain free
column7: No — requires a local MATLAB/Octave install, not browser-based
column10: No fixed battery; low-level stimulus/timing precision toolkit

column6: Easy — GUI builder over Psychtoolbox to reduce coding burden
column7: Not published as a formal open-source repo (distributed as journal supplementary material)
column12: No fixed battery; GUI front-end for Psychtoolbox experiments

column5: Very easy — drag-and-drop, no-code option; integrates with Experiment Factory
column8: Self-hosted (any static web server, GitHub Pages), via the companion Open Lab hosting service, or exported to JATOS
column10: No fixed battery; visual builder for browser studies

column3: partial mirrors only (e.g. https://github.com/danielelozzi/psytoolkit); core engine not fully on GitHub
column6: GPL (per project documentation; the core engine isn't centrally published on GitHub)
column8: Centrally hosted on psytoolkit.org ("professional data centers" per the site), or download and self-host on your own Linux server
column9: Author-defined; library has 50+ ready experiments to copy/modify
column10: Library includes Stroop, Flanker, N-back, IAT-style tasks, personality/survey instruments
